content.assessment: This proposal most explicitly confronts fiscal magnitude and cost incidence by defaulting to closure on transparent viability thresholds while attempting to redistribute the resulting costs (transport, transition, equity support) toward affected pupils rather than leaving them uncompensated. However, its underlying fiscal rationale rests on contested evidence: whether consolidation produces genuine net savings once transportation cost offsets are counted is unresolved (F-live-education_finance-02, F-live-education_finance-03), meaning the premise that closure generates fiscal benefit sufficient to fund robust mitigation is not established. Additionally, the mitigation package (transport, transition support, anti-segregation safeguards) itself represents a nontrivial ongoing cost that could erode or eliminate any net savings, and distributional evidence shows closure disproportionately affects Roma and low-mobility poorer families (F-live-education_finance-06), raising cost-incidence equity concerns that mitigation measures may only partially offset.
opportunities: If paired with a registry-style tracking of realized savings versus mitigation costs, the proposal could generate rigorous evidence on whether default-closure-with-mitigation is fiscally net-positive
strengths: Explicitly attempts to internalize downstream costs (transport, transition disruption, segregation risk) rather than treating them as externalities, improving cost-incidence transparency

Directly engages the distributional finding that closure disproportionately removes options for poorer and Roma families with lower mobility (F-live-education_finance-06), building targeted equity support into the model
threats: Legal ambiguity over transport funding responsibility (Interior Ministry vs. municipalities) creates a resourcing gap that could leave mitigation unfunded or contested in practice

If mitigation is under-resourced relative to statutory promises, the equity and transport safeguards may exist on paper without matching fiscal commitment, replicating documented patterns of under-budgeting
weaknesses: The fiscal case for closure-by-default rests on contested and unresolved evidence about whether net savings actually materialize once transportation costs are counted (F-live-education_finance-02, F-live-education_finance-03)

Comprehensive mitigation (transport, transition support, anti-segregation measures) is itself costly and could offset most or all of the claimed savings, undermining the value-for-money case for consolidation as designed
